title: vSAN Disk Group Health
dataSources: Primary: (index=vmware OR index=hci) and sourcetype=vmware:vsan:diskgroup — JSON from a custom HEC line produced by a Python poller that calls the vCenter REST GET /api/vcenter/vsan/cluster/{cluster}/disk-groups (vSphere 7.0 U2+) and flattens diskGroupUuid (or disk_group_uuid after normalisation), cacheDiskCanonical as cache_disk_canonical, per-disk capacity lists, state in {mounted,unmounted,error,permanent_error}, health, disk_format_version, dedup_compression_enabled, vsan_object_count, diskGroupIoErrors, checksumErrorsTotal split into software and hardware if available, and congestionValue 0–255. Optional union: sourcetype=vmware:vsan:health for host or cluster check summaries. Fallback when REST is not available: vmware:vc:event from the Splunk Add-on for VMware with com.vmware.vsan.* style filters, which is less granular. The vSAN Health Service is also available at GET /api/vcenter/vsan/health for a second poller you name vmware:vsan:health.
app: Choose one or combine: (1) Splunk Add-on for VMware ([Splunkbase 3215](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/3215)) for legacy vCenter streams such as vmware:vc:event; it is deprecated for Splunk Cloud in favor of the forwarder path. (2) Splunk Add-on for VMware vSphere Forwarder ([Splunkbase 5380](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5380)) for the modern vSphere forwarder. (3) Custom HEC with pyVmomi or the vSphere Automation SDK calling GET /api/vcenter/vsan/cluster/{cluster}/disk-groups and GET /api/vcenter/vsan/health because no stock TA emits vmware:vsan:diskgroup. Reference: HEC in Splunk docs, and [github.com/vmware/pyvmomi](https://github.com/vmware/pyvmomi).
spl:
`comment("UC-19.2.17 — vSAN Disk Group Health: per-cluster, per-host, per-DG mount state and checksum trends from vmware:vsan:diskgroup. Templated sourcetype vsan:diskgroup is fictional; this is the custom HEC convention from a pyVmomi poller of vSphere 7.0 U2+ /api/vcenter/vsan/cluster/{cluster}/disk-groups.")`
(index=vmware OR index=hci) (sourcetype="vmware:vsan:diskgroup" OR sourcetype="vmware:vsan:health") earliest=-1h@h
| eval disk_group_uuid=coalesce(diskGroupUuid, disk_group_uuid, "")
| eval state=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(state, stateValue, ""))))
| eval health=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(health, healthStatus, health_state, ""))))
| eval cluster=coalesce(cluster, clusterName, cluster_name, "")
| eval host=coalesce(host, hostName, hostname, esx_host, "")
| eval checksum_errors_total=tonumber(toString(coalesce(checksumErrorsTotal, checksum_errors_total, "0")), 10)
| eval disk_group_io_errors=tonumber(toString(coalesce(diskGroupIoErrors, disk_group_io_errors, "0")), 10)
| eval congestion_value=tonumber(toString(coalesce(congestionValue, congestion_value, "0")), 10)
| where len(disk_group_uuid)>0
| sort 0 + _time, cluster, host, disk_group_uuid
| streamstats window=2 current=f last(checksum_errors_total) as prior_checksum by cluster, host, disk_group_uuid
| eval checksum_delta=if(isnotnull(prior_checksum) AND isnotnull(checksum_errors_total), checksum_errors_total - prior_checksum, 0)
| eval state_severity=case(state=="permanent_error", 4, state=="error", 3, state=="unmounted", 2, 1=1, 0)
| stats max(_time) as _time latest(state) as state latest(health) as health latest(checksum_errors_total) as checksum_errors_total latest(disk_group_io_errors) as disk_group_io_errors latest(congestion_value) as congestion_value latest(checksum_delta) as checksum_delta latest(state_severity) as state_severity by cluster, host, disk_group_uuid
| lookup vsan_cluster_owner cluster OUTPUT business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until, expected_dg_count_per_host
| eval freeze_active=if(isnotnull(change_freeze_until) AND len(change_freeze_until)>0 AND now()<coalesce(strptime(change_freeze_until, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"), strptime(change_freeze_until, "%Y-%m-%d"), 0), 1, 0)
| eval severity_bucket=case((state=="error" OR state=="permanent_error") OR health=="error", "critical", (state=="unmounted" OR checksum_delta>100), "high", health=="unhealthy", "medium", congestion_value>50, "low", 1=1, "ok")
| eval severity_bucket=if(freeze_active=1 AND severity_bucket!="ok", "ok", severity_bucket)
| table _time, severity_bucket, cluster, host, disk_group_uuid, state, health, checksum_delta, checksum_errors_total, business_service, owner_email
description: Monitors per-disk group vSAN state ingested as vmware:vsan:diskgroup (custom HEC from a pyVmomi or vSphere REST poller) with optional vmware:vsan:health rows that share the same cluster and host keys. The SPL coalesces camelCase REST (diskGroupUuid, state, checksumErrorsTotal) and snake_case copies, sorts poll events by time, uses streamstats to build a per-disk group checksum_delta, deduplicates with stats latest() by cluster, host, and disk_group_uuid, joins vsan_cluster_owner for business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until, and expected_dg_count_per_host, evaluates freeze_active to hold pages during a freeze, and assigns severity_bucket from unmount, health, and congestion signals; if no API feed exists yet, compare against vmware:vc:event patterns documented in exclusions.
value: When a disk group leaves the healthy mounted path, virtual machines on that vSAN data store can stall I/O, lose quorum for writes, and fail vMotion; sustained checksum growth points to media or software paths that force rebuild traffic across 10G and 25G uplinks and consume capacity-tier rebuild windows that operators already scheduled for other work. Catching the transition early shortens the blast radius to a few hosts, keeps rebuild parallelization under control, and gives application owners time to move workloads before database clusters fence or Kubernetes volumes become read-only for long periods.
implementation: Build TA_vmware_vsan on a Heavy Forwarder: script:///.../vsan_diskgroup_poll.py every 5m, HEC to index=vmware with sourcetype=vmware:vsan:diskgroup, add lookups/vsan_cluster_owner.csv, then schedule the spl every 5m with throttles. Optionally ingest vmware:vsan:health the same way. Replace index=vmware with index=hci everywhere if that is your standard naming; do not duplicate both in production without a design review.
visualization: Row 1: two single values for critical and high counts. Row 2: timechart of checksum_delta. Row 3: alert table. Row 4: optional overlay with resync use case. Row 5: line chart of checksum_errors_total for trend versus spike detection.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for VMware vSphere Forwarder (Splunkbase 5380)
Splunk: Use the HTTP Event Collector

knownFalsePositives: Planned disk group decommission for a cache device swap can show unmounted while data evacuates, which is legitimate under an approved change. Stretched vSAN failovers may show heavy resync when a site returns, which is not the same as permanent_error on a local NVMe. ESXi maintenance with data evacuation can make remaining hosts look unhealthy briefly as objects move. vSAN major upgrades that rebalance metadata can bump checksum or IO error counters that settle when the cluster finishes. A witness host network blip can look like data-plane disk group issues if routing is misread—verify witness use cases before hardware RMA. A flaky poller that omits fields intermittently is indistinguishable from a true fault without dead-letter logging.
hardware: vSAN ReadyNode and OEM HCI servers include Dell PowerEdge R640, R740, and R750 class two-socket nodes with direct-attached bays, HPE ProLiant DL360 and DL380, Cisco UCS C240, Lenovo ThinkSystem SR650, and Supermicro SuperStorage lines. Each disk group uses a cache tier of enterprise NVMe or write-intensive SSDs in front of a capacity tier of read-optimized SATA SSDs or 7.2K NL-SAS drives. Keep firmware on the VMware compatibility list. Rebuilds stress both tiers and upstream 10/25/100G links; this text describes physical patterns only—the logic lives in the saved search and poller field contract.
exclusions: The templated sourcetype vsan:diskgroup is fictional; the real name is vmware:vsan:diskgroup from custom HEC, or a normalized line derived from vmware:vc:event filtered to vSAN event families such as com.vmware.vsan.* and esx.audit.vsan.* when a REST poller is not in place. This UC is not witness networking alone (see UC-19.2.23), not capacity free space (see UC-19.2.27), and not component resync backlogs (see UC-19.2.30). It does not replace vendor Skyline emails; it is your Splunk-side detector for mount, health, and checksums. Do not run this unchanged against oVirt, Nutanix, or Proxmox data models.
evidence: Saved report uc_19_2_17_vsan_diskgroup_health on index=vmware or index=hci with vmware:vsan:diskgroup, vsan_cluster_owner lookup enrichment, and alert history for operations evidence packs.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• vCenter 7.0 U2 or later with the vSphere REST vSAN cluster disk group endpoint reachable from your collection host, OR an older vCenter where you will fall back to the vSAN Management Object Browser utility path and pyVmomi vim.cluster.VsanInternalSystem patterns documented in VMware KB threads for your release train.
• A Heavy Forwarder or other Linux host that can run Python 3.9+ with the pyVmomi wheel installed from PyPI, outbound HTTPS to https://<vcenter-fqdn> on 443, and a separate Splunk HEC or intermediate indexer on 8088 (TLS recommended).
• Service account with the minimum VMware Cloud Administrator–equivalent read scopes for the vCenter REST namespace that exposes GET /api/vcenter/vsan/cluster/{cluster}/disk-groups and (recommended) GET /api/vcenter/vsan/health for the same cluster IDs you enumerate.
• Indexes vmware and/or hci created in Splunk, HEC or HTTP Event Collector input allowing those index names, and a Search Head app containing lookups/vsan_cluster_owner.csv (see Step 1) plus transforms.conf to expose the vsan_cluster_owner lookup by filename.
• Baseline idea of how many disk group objects each cache tier and capacity tier host should publish so an inventory gap is explainable: store expected_dg_count_per_host in the cluster owner lookup or derive it from a CMDB join.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection (custom HEC, first-class vSAN field fidelity)**
Contract: land events as **`vmware:vsan:diskgroup`** using **`pyVmomi`** or the vSphere REST session so each **disk group** shows its **cache tier** and **capacity tier** drives under the same JSON line. The templated name vsan:diskgroup seen in some boilerplate packs is not a real Splunk-sourced event type. Your deployment should emit vmware:vsan:diskgroup (one JSON line per disk group per poll) so field names in this use case are stable. If you only have VMware TA events today, the complementary path is vmware:vc:event with eventTypeId or message keys matching vSAN, but the REST poll path below is the one that populates per-disk group state, health, and checksum counters in one row.

1A. HEC on the index tier — example [http://vmware_hci_hec] stanza: enable TLS, restrict indexes to vmware and hci, and note the token in your password vault, not a repo. Set useACK = 1 for production if your forwarder can retry.

1B. Polling design — for each vCenter cluster, resolve the vSAN cluster identifier your REST call expects (vSphere 7+ REST uses a cluster name parameter in the path; older builds may need the cluster MoRef you gather once through com.vmware.vcenter.Cluster.get). A minimal Python loop: enumerate clusters tagged for vSAN, call GET https://<vcenter>/api/vcenter/vsan/cluster/{cluster}/disk-groups with a session or OAuth bearer from the vSphere Automation session helper, then flatten each array element: retain camelCase for raw JSON, but the poller also emits snake_case copies so the Splunk coalesce chain matches both shapes.

1C. Sample field mapping from REST — keep diskGroupUuid, cacheDiskCanonical, state, and capacity-disk lists on the HEC line; for checksums, if the base disk-groups payload omits checksum_errors_total, merge in values from a companion call to the same host’s vSAN object-level stats or the Health API JSON where VMware exposes the aggregate, and do not double-count. Document which branch your code used in props notes for future engineers.

1D. lookups/vsan_cluster_owner.csv minimum columns: cluster, business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until (empty when none), expected_dg_count_per_host (optional numeric string). The lookup lets operators suppress or reroute when finance systems freeze changes over quarter close even though hardware is blinking red.

1E. transforms.conf for the filename-based lookup: [vsan_cluster_owner] filename = vsan_cluster_owner.csv with case_sensitive_match = false and min_matches = 1 and default_match = blank so you can see missing service rows. Wire an automatic search-time lookup only if you are comfortable; this UC already calls lookup explicitly in the alert SPL.

1F. props.conf for vmware:vsan:diskgroup (Heavy Forwarder or indexer that parses the line):
```ini
[vmware:vsan:diskgroup]
TRUNCATE = 2000000
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
LINE_BREAKER = ([\r\n]+)
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json
MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD = 32
TZ = UTC
```
If you add TIME_PREFIX to pull _time from a JSON poll_utc field, do so; otherwise the HEC receipt time is acceptable for 60–300s poll cadence after you document the trade-off.

1G. When you cannot yet reach the disk-groups API on an older 6.7 stretch cluster, the fallback is still pyVmomi: use content.sh in the vSAN MOB, translate VsanStoragePoolHealthSummary and disk-group objects into the same vmware:vsan:diskgroup JSON shape. Keep one contract even when two code paths run behind a feature flag.

1H. The optional vmware:vsan:health stream should align cluster and host keys so a future join or append can merge; events without a diskGroupUuid are ignored by the where len(disk_group_uuid)>0 guard in the saved search, which is intentional—those health snapshots belong in a different dashboard while you enrich the per-DG series.

**Step 2 — Create the search, alert, and story for operators**
Run the SPL in Search, save as a report first, then clone to a scheduled alert at the period that matches your poll interval. Recommended schedule: if you poll every five minutes, run the search every five minutes on earliest=-1h@h so a checksum burst across two adjacent polls is visible. Throttle the alert on cluster, host, and disk_group_uuid to prevent duplicate pages during flapping unmounts.

Understanding the SPL: the comment wrapper records intent for Splunk audit exports without changing results. The base index clause accepts either vmware or hci because some enterprises reserve hci for all hyperconverged streams; pick one in production to avoid duplicate event volume, not both. The sourcetype union lets you stage vmware:vsan:health alongside per-DG rows even though the where clause keeps only events that carry a non-empty diskGroupUuid after coalesce. The coalesce chain tolerates vSphere’s camelCase exports (diskGroupUuid) and snake_case you might emit from Python. The streamstats window compares this poll to the previous poll in the 1h window so checksum_delta is not lifetime cumulative unless that is the only data you have, in which case you should also ingest a poll_seq to reset counts when the poller restarts. state_severity is a numeric assist if you add executive dashboards. The lookup enriches ownership, then freeze_active mutes severity_bucket to ok for paging while still keeping raw columns if you add them back in a mirror alert. The case for severity_bucket encodes: disk-group error and permanent_error and health=error are critical, any unmounted or checksum delta above 100 in a poll is high, health=unhealthy is medium, and elevated congestion_value (vSAN backpressure) over 50 is low until you confirm sustained load from another panel.

Alert trigger expression: severity_bucket in the set {critical, high, medium} for production databases, and include low only for edge clusters with explicit sign-off, because a single high congestion read may be a backup spike. Use summary indexing if you need month-over-month trends without rescanning raw events.

```spl
`comment("UC-19.2.17 — vSAN Disk Group Health: per-cluster, per-host, per-DG mount state and checksum trends from vmware:vsan:diskgroup. Templated sourcetype vsan:diskgroup is fictional; this is the custom HEC convention from a pyVmomi poller of vSphere 7.0 U2+ /api/vcenter/vsan/cluster/{cluster}/disk-groups.")`
(index=vmware OR index=hci) (sourcetype="vmware:vsan:diskgroup" OR sourcetype="vmware:vsan:health") earliest=-1h@h
| eval disk_group_uuid=coalesce(diskGroupUuid, disk_group_uuid, "")
| eval state=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(state, stateValue, ""))))
| eval health=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(health, healthStatus, health_state, ""))))
| eval cluster=coalesce(cluster, clusterName, cluster_name, "")
| eval host=coalesce(host, hostName, hostname, esx_host, "")
| eval checksum_errors_total=tonumber(toString(coalesce(checksumErrorsTotal, checksum_errors_total, "0")), 10)
| eval disk_group_io_errors=tonumber(toString(coalesce(diskGroupIoErrors, disk_group_io_errors, "0")), 10)
| eval congestion_value=tonumber(toString(coalesce(congestionValue, congestion_value, "0")), 10)
| where len(disk_group_uuid)>0
| sort 0 + _time, cluster, host, disk_group_uuid
| streamstats window=2 current=f last(checksum_errors_total) as prior_checksum by cluster, host, disk_group_uuid
| eval checksum_delta=if(isnotnull(prior_checksum) AND isnotnull(checksum_errors_total), checksum_errors_total - prior_checksum, 0)
| eval state_severity=case(state=="permanent_error", 4, state=="error", 3, state=="unmounted", 2, 1=1, 0)
| stats max(_time) as _time latest(state) as state latest(health) as health latest(checksum_errors_total) as checksum_errors_total latest(disk_group_io_errors) as disk_group_io_errors latest(congestion_value) as congestion_value latest(checksum_delta) as checksum_delta latest(state_severity) as state_severity by cluster, host, disk_group_uuid
| lookup vsan_cluster_owner cluster OUTPUT business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until, expected_dg_count_per_host
| eval freeze_active=if(isnotnull(change_freeze_until) AND len(change_freeze_until)>0 AND now()<coalesce(strptime(change_freeze_until, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"), strptime(change_freeze_until, "%Y-%m-%d"), 0), 1, 0)
| eval severity_bucket=case((state=="error" OR state=="permanent_error") OR health=="error", "critical", (state=="unmounted" OR checksum_delta>100), "high", health=="unhealthy", "medium", congestion_value>50, "low", 1=1, "ok")
| eval severity_bucket=if(freeze_active=1 AND severity_bucket!="ok", "ok", severity_bucket)
| table _time, severity_bucket, cluster, host, disk_group_uuid, state, health, checksum_delta, checksum_errors_total, business_service, owner_email
```

**Step 3 — Validate (vendor UI, math, and lookup honesty)**
1. vSphere Client — navigate to the vSAN cluster, **Monitor → vSAN → Disk Management** or Health, find the same disk group UUID; compare state and any health band to the newest indexed row. 2. If you run Skyline or vSAN Health in vCenter, open the vSAN object health for the same host and make sure the timestamp is within the Splunk poll_utc skew you allow (usually <10 minutes). 3. In Splunk, run a raw check: | stats count by cluster, host, disk_group_uuid over -1h to ensure the cardinality matches what vCenter shows; a missing disk group on one host is often a failed poll, not a silent disk removal. 4. | inputlookup vsan_cluster_owner.csv to confirm each production cluster has an owner, and that change_freeze_until parses with the same strptime you used. 5. NTP: compare vCenter, ESXi, and the Heavy Forwarder; minutes of skew can reorder streamstats. 6. If checksums reset to zero on upgrade, file a one-time maintenance so alerts do not interpret the drop as a negative delta.

**Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard, routing, and evidence)**
Dashboard layout recommendation: first row, single value tiles for counts of severity_bucket in critical and high, filtered to production clusters. Second row, heatmap of host by congestion_value for last hour. Third row, table sorted by checksum_delta descending, then a drilldown to disk group detail in vSphere using a drilldown link pattern your local portal documents. Add a line chart of checksum_errors_total for the noisiest disk group to separate steady noise from a step change. For ServiceNow, map owner_email as assignment group hint and attach cluster and disk_group_uuid in the short description. For evidence, export the saved search history and keep the JSON poller stdout in your change record when you tune thresholds. Pair this UC with physical disk and NVMe workstreams so capacity-tier replacements do not drown in duplicate stories—this UC is about the mount state and checksums, not full-object resync depth (see UC-19.2.30) and not witness path loss (see UC-19.2.23) or capacity free space (see UC-19.2.27).

**Step 5 — Troubleshoot (ingest, SPL, and vSAN edge cases)**
Empty index: the scripted input is not running on the Heavy Forwarder, the HEC token is rejected, or the service account cannot see the vSAN API namespace—check ExecProcessor and 401 in splunkd.log. checksum_delta is always zero: only one event per disk group in the time window, or checksum fields not extracted—tighten poll frequency or import cumulative checksum fields from health. severity_bucket is always ok but vCenter is red: your JSON path might map HEALTHY to lowercase incorrectly; add a temporary | eval _dbg=raw_state to compare. freeze_active is stuck on: the clock format in the CSV is wrong—fix strptime to include timezone offsets if you use them. lookup does not return business_service: the cluster string must match the CSV cluster exactly, including case; normalize in the poller. Congestion is perpetually low while vSAN is fine: 50 is a starting threshold—raise in all-flash clusters with bursty writes only after you plot two weeks. API rate limits: cache cluster lists for 24h and only refresh disk groups every poll. TLS errors: add your enterprise root to the poller’s trust store, not a global insecure flag in production. When VMware deprecates a REST minor version, re-run this validation section before your next LCM change freeze.

End-state guidance: vSAN uses cache flash in front of capacity drives; when rebuilds run, 10/25/100G links fill quickly—pair congestion_value with top-of-rack error counters from your network monitoring. HEC tokens belong in a vault, never a repo. When multiple vCenters share similar cluster names, prefix cluster in both the poller and the lookup. Governance teams should file this report beside change tickets so a checksum spike during a code freeze is explainable. If you outgrow CSV lookups, port vsan_cluster_owner to KV store with the same column names. Stretched vSAN and witness designs need correlated witness use cases; do not assume every mount flap is a bad NVMe. grandmaExplanation: We watch the storage building blocks on each host in your private cloud so we know if a bundle goes offline, misreports health, or flips checksums. When that happens, programs on top can freeze or fail to move, and repair work can fill the network. We help the right team respond before a small storage issue becomes a long outage for everyday services.

controlTest.positiveScenario: In a lab, fault a disk group to error or unmount; two poll cycles later Splunk should show a non-ok severity_bucket and the alert should list that disk group with owner fields from the lookup when cluster matches.
controlTest.negativeScenario: With change_freeze_until in the past or empty, freeze_active stays zero; with a valid future freeze timestamp that parses, the same raw fault is downgraded to ok for paging while you still have the raw state in a parallel unfiltered report.
